From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

This article is about the British television series. For the song recorded by the Mamas & the Papas, see [Monday, Monday](/wiki/Monday,_Monday "Monday, Monday"). For the album by Paul Horn, see [Monday, Monday (album)](/wiki/Monday,_Monday_(album) "Monday, Monday (album)").

2009 British TV series or programme

| *Monday Monday* | |
| --- | --- |

| Release | 13 July (2009-07-13) – 24 August 2009 (2009-08-24) |

***Monday Monday*** is an [ITV](/wiki/ITV_(TV_network) "ITV (TV network)") [comedy drama](/wiki/Comedy_drama "Comedy drama"). It stars [Fay Ripley](/wiki/Fay_Ripley "Fay Ripley"), [Jenny Agutter](/wiki/Jenny_Agutter "Jenny Agutter"), [Neil Stuke](/wiki/Neil_Stuke "Neil Stuke"), [Holly Aird](/wiki/Holly_Aird "Holly Aird"), [Morven Christie](/wiki/Morven_Christie "Morven Christie"), [Tom Ellis](/wiki/Tom_Ellis_(actor) "Tom Ellis (actor)"), and [Miranda Hart](/wiki/Miranda_Hart "Miranda Hart").

It is set in the head office of a [supermarket](/wiki/Supermarket "Supermarket") that has fallen on hard times and had to re-locate its staff from [London](/wiki/London "London") to [Leeds](/wiki/Leeds "Leeds").[[2]](#cite_note-broadcast-20071114-2) The show was initially announced as part of ITV's Winter 2007 press pack, but was "iced" until 2009 due to falling advertising in the wake of the economic downturn.[[2]](#cite_note-broadcast-20071114-2)[[3]](#cite_note-broadcast-20090211-3)

## Background

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monday_Monday&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Background")]

The show is named after [The Mamas & the Papas](/wiki/The_Mamas_%26_the_Papas "The Mamas & the Papas") song [with the same name](/wiki/Monday,_Monday "Monday, Monday"), though does not feature the song as a theme tune.

The show was commissioned by ITV's director of drama, Laura Mackie.[[1]](#cite_note-presspack-1) Mackie said that the show aimed to "entertain, engage and strike a chord with the audience".[[1]](#cite_note-presspack-1) Although the show was initially announced as part of ITV's Winter 2007 press pack, it was put on hold until 2009 due to falling advertising rates in the wake of the economic downturn.[[2]](#cite_note-broadcast-20071114-2)[[3]](#cite_note-broadcast-20090211-3) According to *[Broadcast](/wiki/Broadcast_(magazine) "Broadcast (magazine)")*, the show aimed to "shed light on a world of alcoholic HR bosses, power-crazed managers and sexually unfettered PAs".[[2]](#cite_note-broadcast-20071114-2)

Towards the end of the first episode, Christine is shown to be an alcoholic, which she eventually admits. As the series progresses the failing life of Christine becomes more central to the plot.

## Plot

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monday_Monday&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Plot")]

The show features a group of head office workers for struggling [supermarket](/wiki/Supermarket "Supermarket") chain *Butterworths.*[[4]](#cite_note-officialwebsite-4) As a result of downsizing, the workers are forced to move cities, relocating from [London](/wiki/London "London") to [Leeds](/wiki/Leeds "Leeds").[[2]](#cite_note-broadcast-20071114-2)

## Cast

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monday_Monday&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Cast")]

**Management**

* [Clive Merrison](/wiki/Clive_Merrison "Clive Merrison") – Gavin, chairman, Butterworths Group
* [Peter Wight](/wiki/Peter_Wight_(actor) "Peter Wight (actor)") – Roger Sorsby, Chief Executive of Butterworth's Stores
* [Jenny Agutter](/wiki/Jenny_Agutter "Jenny Agutter") – Jenny Mountfield, PA to Roger Sorsby
* [Holly Aird](/wiki/Holly_Aird "Holly Aird") – Alyson Cartmell, Chief Operating Officer
* [Tom Ellis](/wiki/Tom_Ellis_(actor) "Tom Ellis (actor)") – Steven McColl, PA to Alyson Cartmell

**Human Resources**

* [Fay Ripley](/wiki/Fay_Ripley "Fay Ripley") – Christine Frances, Head of Human Resources
* [Morven Christie](/wiki/Morven_Christie "Morven Christie") – Sally Newman, PA to Christine
* [Miranda Hart](/wiki/Miranda_Hart "Miranda Hart") – Tall Karen, Office Assistant
* Jodie Taibi – Small Karen, Office Assistant

**Marketing**

* [Neil Stuke](/wiki/Neil_Stuke "Neil Stuke") – Max Chambers, Acting Head of Marketing
* [Laura Haddock](/wiki/Laura_Haddock "Laura Haddock") – Natasha Wright, Marketing Assistant (prev. PA to Vivienne Wyatt. With Vivienne off sick recovering from cancer, Natasha is seconded to Max Chambers)
* [Saikat Ahamed](/wiki/Saikat_Ahamed "Saikat Ahamed") – Vince, PA to Max Chambers & Natasha Wright (prev. PA to Max Chambers)

**Finance**

* Nick Sampson – Keith Saunders, Head of Finance

**Building Services & Facilities Management**

* Joan Oliver – Susan, Facilities Manager
* Susan Earl – Janet, Facilities Assistant

**Shop Floor**

* [Sue Vincent](/wiki/Sue_Vincent "Sue Vincent") – Helen, Bakery Manager
* Bruce McGregor – Greg, Fish Manager
* [Richard Fleeshman](/wiki/Richard_Fleeshman "Richard Fleeshman") – Gillon, Bakery Customer Service Assistant

The departments named above are the main departments focused on but other departments are mentioned or featured on presentations. They include:

* Contracts
* Sourcing
* IT
* Design
* Property
* International Sales
* National Sales

## Butterworths Group PLC

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monday_Monday&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Butterworths Group PLC")]

Butterworths Head Office

The Head Office is arranged into a North Wing and a South Wing on all floors above the Lower Ground Floor.

* Lower Ground: Canteen, Building Services/Property/Facilities Management & Sourcing
* Ground Floor: Marketing (South) Meeting Area (North) & Reception/Security in Centre
* First Floor: Human Resources (South) Contracts (North)
* Second Floor: International Sales (South) Finance & Chief Executive's Office (North)
* Third Floor: National Sales (South) Design (North)

Other Services than Retail are Insurance Sales. Each department is managed by a Department Head and each department head has a personal assistant

## Broadcast and reception

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monday_Monday&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Broadcast and reception")]

The show did not receive good reviews. Tim Walker of *[The Independent](/wiki/The_Independent "The Independent")* stated that the shows rival in the same timeslot, [BBC One](/wiki/BBC_One "BBC One") show *[The Street](/wiki/The_Street_(UK_TV_series) "The Street (UK TV series)")* was "a darn sight more interesting than watching Fay Ripley walk into doors". Walker said that the show was filled with shots of [Leeds](/wiki/Leeds "Leeds"), saying that the show was purely "advertising it as an attractive nightlife destination" or "to prove beyond doubt that ITV was fulfilling its obligations to the regions." (something that may have been fitting with ITV mothballing many parts of its [Leeds Studios](/wiki/Leeds_Studios "Leeds Studios") the same year).[[5]](#cite_note-independent-5) Sam Wollaston of *[The Guardian](/wiki/The_Guardian "The Guardian")* also criticised the show saying that it was "lame and laboured, tired and predictable".[[6]](#cite_note-6)

The poor reception was reflected in the show's ratings, the show bringing in 3.7 million, a 16% viewing share, six per cent and 1.3 million viewers lower than *The Street*. The show, however, was second in its timeslot, beating the other three [terrestrial](/wiki/Terrestrial_television "Terrestrial television") channels in the slot.[[7]](#cite_note-7) Despite being second in the timeslot, the show was down on the 5.6 million for ITV1's channel slot average so far in 2009.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

Not helping matters in terms of viewing figures was that [STV](/wiki/STV_(TV_network) "STV (TV network)") continued its recent trend of declining high-ticket ITV productions, thereby avoiding having to contribute to the production cost on a pro rata basis, and so Scottish viewers of terrestrial broadcasts were unable to view the series. The series average, based on overnight ratings was 2.90m, equating to a 13.2% viewing share.

| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |

| Episode 2 | 20 July 2009 | 3.40 | 15.0% | 3.20 | 24 | [[11]](#cite_note-11) |
| Episode 3 | 27 July 2009 | 2.62 | 12.1% | n/a | 30+ | [[12]](#cite_note-12) |
| Episode 4 | 3 August 2009 | 2.63 | 12.2% | n/a | 30+ | [[13]](#cite_note-13) |
| Episode 5 | 10 August 2009 | 2.68 | 12.3% | 2.69 | 28 | [[14]](#cite_note-14) |
| Episode 6 | 17 August 2009 | 2.47 | 11.3% | 2.54 | 29 | [[15]](#cite_note-15) |
| Episode 7 | 24 August 2009 | 2.84 | 13.2% | 2.86 | 24 | [[16]](#cite_note-16) |

## International broadcasts

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monday_Monday&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: International broadcasts")]

In [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia"), this programme aired each Friday at 8:30pm on [ABC2](/wiki/ABC2 "ABC2") from 28 January 2011.[[17]](#cite_note-17)
In [Serbia](/wiki/Serbia "Serbia"), this programme aired on Fox Life. In the United States the show is on Hulu.com and currently available via Netflix and YouTube.
